voiceover: Your website had visitors while you were gone. Real people. Real questions. And your site just... stood there. Like a mascot waving at traffic while inside, a chat box blinks with nothing to say. They didn't fill out your form. They left. That's not a vacation problem — that's every night, every weekend, every holiday. Your pipeline leaks while you sleep.
title: The Mascot Who Suffered While Your Website Said Nothing
concept: Open on an absurd but immediately readable scene: a person in a full mascot costume standing in a sun-baked parking lot, waving at passing cars — sweating, suffering, performing. Cut inside: a chat widget on a website screen blinks a typing indicator... then nothing. No message ever arrives. Widen to an after-hours office, lights off, phone silent, analytics ticking up with visitor counts nobody sees. The twist: this isn't a vacation story — it's every single night. Close with a quiet, confident payoff: an AI chatbot answering what the mascot never could.
script:
BEAT 1 — HOOK (0–3s): Mascot in a parking lot, waving at traffic. Voiceover: 'Mascot suffers, fake typing online.' Bold, clipped, urgent. SFX click accent at ~335ms.

BEAT 2 — IMPLICATION (3–10s): Cut inside. Chat widget blinks. Typing indicator pulses. No message. Voiceover: 'Your website had visitors while you were gone. Real people. Real questions. And your site just... stood there.'

BEAT 3 — TWIST / ESCALATION (10–18s): Wide shot — dark office, lights off, visitor counter climbing. Voiceover: 'They didn't fill out your form. They left. That's not a vacation problem — that's every night, every weekend, every holiday.'

BEAT 4 — PAYOFF / CTA (18–23s): Calm co-working space, morning light. Someone opens a laptop; a chat conversation is already there — answered overnight. Voiceover: 'Your pipeline leaks while you sleep.' Soft pause. 'Create your AI assistant at fenrik.chat.'
image_prompts: Vertical 9:16 portrait frame. A bright co-working space exterior — a sunlit parking lot adjacent to a glass-fronted office. A single person in a large, slightly deflated mascot costume (animal character, no readable text on costume) stands at the edge of the lot, arm raised mid-wave toward passing traffic. The costume is visibly warm — fabric creased, posture slumped. Flat documentary lighting, warm neutral color feel, wide environmental framing with layered depth: mascot in foreground, blurred cars and building in background. Candid, slightly absurd, immediately readable. No text, no signs, no readable labels in the image.

Vertical 9:16 portrait frame. Interior of a bright co-working space in daylight. A desktop monitor on a standing desk shows a website chat widget — the typing indicator (three animated dots) pulses on screen, but the chat window is otherwise empty, no message ever arrives. A single person stands partially in frame at the left edge, back half-turned, not looking at the screen. Flat, low-contrast documentary lighting, warm neutral tones. Wide environmental framing, layered depth — foreground desk, background open co-working area softly blurred. No readable text, no legible UI labels, no metrics visible on screen.

Vertical 9:16 portrait frame. A bright co-working space at dusk — overhead lights off, natural light fading through large windows. A single desk with a closed notebook and a silent phone. On a wall-mounted monitor in the background, a website analytics dashboard glows faintly — abstract bar shapes and rising line visible but no readable numbers or text. The space feels quietly abandoned, visitor traffic accumulating unseen. Flat documentary lighting with warm-to-cool transition, wide environmental framing, layered depth. One partial human silhouette visible through a distant glass partition. No readable text or labels.

Vertical 9:16 portrait frame. A bright co-working space early morning — warm daylight flooding in through wide windows. A single person, partial body visible from the shoulder down, opens a laptop at a standing desk. The laptop screen shows a chat conversation already populated with exchanges — abstract message bubbles, no readable text — answered overnight while the office was empty. A coffee cup sits nearby, steam rising. Flat, low-contrast documentary lighting, warm neutral color feel, wide environmental framing with foreground desk and softly blurred background workspace. Candid, hopeful, immediately readable. No legible text or UI labels on screen.

linkedin.caption: An accountant came back from two weeks off to find that several small business owners had visited her website asking about tax filing deadlines. None of them filled out the contact form. None left an email. They moved on. This is not a vacation problem. It is a structural one. Every business that relies on a static website and a contact form is running a pipeline with a permanent leak — one that opens every night, every weekend, and every public holiday. The visitors arrive. The questions go unanswered. The leads disappear. The fix does not require a developer or a custom build. It requires a website that can actually respond when you cannot.
linkedin.cta: What does your website say to a visitor at 11 PM? Drop your answer below.

title_variants: Your website is open 24/7. Your answers are not.
The silent pipeline leak most small businesses never notice
caption_variants: An accountant came back from two weeks off to find that several small business owners had visited her website asking about tax filing deadlines. None of them filled out the contact form. None left an email. They moved on. This is not a vacation problem. It is a structural one. Every business that relies on a static website and a contact form is running a pipeline with a permanent leak — one that opens every night, every weekend, and every public holiday. The visitors arrive. The questions go unanswered. The leads disappear. The fix does not require a developer or a custom build. It requires a website that can actually respond when you cannot.

Most businesses assume they lose leads because of price, competition, or the wrong audience. The real reason is quieter: a visitor landed on the site at 10 PM, had a genuine question, found no way to get an answer, and left. No form filled. No email sent. No trace. This happens every night. It happened last weekend. It will happen tonight. A contact form is not a support channel. It is a waiting room with no staff. The businesses winning on lead capture right now are the ones whose websites can hold a conversation when the team cannot.
